coredata.dc:title: Paper sensor of curcumin by fluorescence resonance energy transfer on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Xiaoyan
Wen, Guangming
Li, Zhongping
Li, Hung-Wing
coredata.dc:description: Paper Sensor detection methods are attractive in wide analytical applications. Presented herein is a paper sensor and fluorescence methods that was firstly developed to detect curcumin (Cur) based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and Cur. The facile fluorescent method was demonstrated to detect Cur in the range of 0–2600 μM with a detection limit of 0.13 μM. And facile paper sensor of Cur was fabricated and displayed at concentration of 0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM, 400 μM, 500 μM and 600 μM, respectively. In additions, it was realized for determination of Cur in real samples including orange juice and curry solution. Compared with the reported methods, the present method is simple, rapid and sensitive for detecting Cur.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Curcumin (Cur)
Paper-based sensor
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Paper-based sensor and fluorescence methods for rapid detection of Cur

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of NCQDs (Inset: particle size distribution). (B) FTIR spectra of NCQDs. (C) The XRD pattern of NCQDs. (D) The XPS spectra of NCQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (E), N 1s (F) and O 1s (G).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cur (a), NCQDs/Cur (b), NCQDs (c). (B) Excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of NCQDs. (C) Emission spectra with various excitation wavelength. (D) Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs (a) and NCQDs/Cur (b).

(A) The effect of UV lamp illumination duration on the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs. (B) The effect of storage time on the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs. and (C) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs.

(A) Fluorescence response of NCQDs to Cur and other selected analogues. (B) Schematic diagram illustrating the FRET mechanism of NCQDs and Cur. (C) The absorption spectrum of Cur (a) and the emission spectrum of the NCQDs (b). (D) The emission spectrum of the NCQDs (a), NCQDs/folic acid (b); folic acid (c); NCQDs/Cur (d); Cur/folic acid (e); and Cur (f).
Emission spectra of the system upon addition of various amount of Cur. The inset was the linear plot of I/I0 versus the concentration of Cur.

(A) Direct visualization in the NCQDs solution including different concentrations of Cur (0 μM, 300 μM, 600 μM, 900 μM, 1200 μM, 1500 μM, 1800 μM, 2100 μM, 2400 μM, respectively). (B) A paper sensor of Cur with different concentrations (The concentration of Cur was 0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM, 400 μM, 500 μM, 600 μM). (C) Selectivity test for Cur. (D) Detection of Cur in curry powder (The concentration of Cur was 0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, as the color darkens).
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of NCQDs.
Schematic of the newly developed paper-based sensor of curcumin.
Results of the Cur recovery experiments performed in orange juice.

head.ce:title: Paper sensor of curcumin by fluorescence resonance energy transfer on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Paper Sensor detection methods are attractive in wide analytical applications. Presented herein is a paper sensor and fluorescence methods that was firstly developed to detect curcumin (Cur) based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and Cur. The facile fluorescent method was demonstrated to detect Cur in the range of 0–2600 μM with a detection limit of 0.13 μM. And facile paper sensor of Cur was fabricated and displayed at concentration of 0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM, 400 μM, 500 μM and 600 μM, respectively. In additions, it was realized for determination of Cur in real samples including orange juice and curry solution. Compared with the reported methods, the present method is simple, rapid and sensitive for detecting Cur.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials in addition to traditional organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots. They are an attractive alternative for their strong photoluminescence, excellent water solubility, anti-photobleaching, chemical stability, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness [1–8]. Various approaches have been developed for the preparation of CQDs with desirable properties for applications in chemical analysis [9–11]. The preparation methods of CQDs can be divided into two categories: top-down method and bottom-up method. The top-down method is effective in luminescence by breaking the precursor of carbon by means of passivation of the polymer surface, including laser ablation [12], electrochemical synthesis [13] and arc discharge [14]. The bottom-up method directly synthesizes fluorescent CQDs by pyrolysis or carbonization of a suitable precursor, including pyrolysis [15], hydrothermal [16], microwave [17], and ultrasonic methods [18]. Among them, the hydrothermal method for the preparation of CQDs involves a reaction of a closed system at suitable temperature and pressure, without the use of strong acid. Moreover, CQDs of high fluorescence quantum yield can be obtained in a one-step reaction without further oxidation of the CQDs, and the preparation method is relatively simple and easily controllable. Especially, doped CQDs have stronger luminescence properties than pure CQDs.

Curcumin [1,7-bis(4-hydroxyl-3-methoxyphenyl)-1,6-heptadiene-3,5-dione], a spice extracted from the root of Curcuma longa Linn, has been being used as a natural coloring matter in food processing. Cur displays strong fluorescence in organic solvents. In the past two decades, numerous studies evidenced that Cur has antitumor, antioxidant, antiarthritic, antiamyloid, anti-ischemic, and anti-inflammatory properties, and it is currently under clinical trials in humans [19–21]. Therefore, quantification of the active ingredient, Cur, is particularly interesting and important in biomedicine and food field. At present, there are only two main methods for detecting Cur. They are Ultra Performance Liquid Chromatography (UPLC) method [22] and conventional fluorometric method [23]. Quite a few reports on the detection of curcumin by fluorescence have been reported. Wahlstrom and Blennow reported a study on the fate of Cur in the rat by fluorometric measurement [24]. Navas Diaz group demonstrated fluorometric determination of Cur in yogurt and mustard [25]. Although these methods possess their own advantages, they also show some shortcomings, such as being expensive and requirement of sophisticated instrument or tedious sample extraction procedures. These detection methods are moderately sensitive and selective. Since Cur has a wide range of applications in field of biomedical and health food, for its anti-tumor, anti-oxidant, anti-arthritic, anti-amyloid, anti-ischemic and anti-inflammatory effects, it is necessary to develop a method for rapid detection of Cur. A simple, rapid, and sensitive method for Cur determination should be established. In this regard, paper sensor visual inspection method is the ideal candidate for detecting Cur.

Herein, we designed a novel paper sensor and fluorescence method for the detection of Cur. Compared with the reported method for detecting Cur, ours has high sensitivity and selectivity in both fluorescence and paper-based sensing methods. In particular, visual detection of Cur by a paper based sensor is being reported for the first time. The detection of Cur is in brief based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between NCQDs and Cur. A facile and low-cost hydrothermal process was applied for the preparation of NCQDs using citric acid and urea as raw materials. The as-prepared NCQDs show peak emission wavelength at 465 nm with excellent biocompatibility, satisfactory stability and photo-bleaching resistance. Remarkably, it was demonstrated that Cur can be detected by naked eyes using a paper based sensor. This method is simple, fast, good selectivity and low cost.

2,4-Dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), tetracycline (Tc), dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), picric acid (PA), phenol, 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), tyrosine (Tyr), aspartic acid (Asp), cysteine (Cys) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp (Beijing, China). Absolute ethanol, curcumin, folic acid, citric acid, urea, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co Ltd (Shanghai, China). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity 18.2 M cm−1 (Bedford, MA, USA). All the reagents were used upon purchased without further purification. DDI water was used in experiments.

The morphology and phase of the NCQDs were characterized with a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin, USA) and an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Japan), respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer system (FTIR, Nicolet Is5, United Kingdom), and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRA DLD, United Kingdom) were used to characterize the chemical composition and functional groups of the NCQDs. UV absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-265 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured with the Hitachi F4500 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). Both the excitation and emission slits were set at 2.5 nm.

In brief, NCQDs was synthesized by the hydrothermal method as follows (Scheme 1 ): 0.1 g of citric acid and 0.7 g of urea was dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 2 min to form a homogeneous mixture solution. Then, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-equipped stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, it was cooled down to room temperature. The resulting solution was filtered through a microporous membrane having a pore size of 0.22 μm, and then centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 20 min and followed by dialyzing for 24 h to remove impurities to obtain NCQDs. The resulting NCQDs solution was stored at 4 °C and used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based sensor and fluorescence methods for rapid detection of Cur
ce:section.ce:para: Paper sensor for rapid detection of Cur. The medium-speed qualitative filter paper with a diameter of 1 cm was immersed in 1 mL of NCQDs solution, and the filter paper was taken out after 30 s. Then the test papers were then dried naturally. 500 μL of a 0 μM (or 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM, 400 μM, 500 μM, 600 μM) Cur solution was added dropwise to the prepared test paper. After drying naturally, the paper sensor was irradiated with ultraviolet light at 365 nm. We use the above test paper as a standard color chart (Scheme 2 ).

Fluorescence method for the detection of Cur. NCQDs solution (200 μL) was dispersed in 700 μL of PBS buffer. Then 100 μL of Cur (or other substance) solution with known concentration was added. The mixture was shaken for 30 s before measuring the emission spectrum (λex = 340 nm).

To investigate the potential in practical application of sensing strategy, standard addition method was used to detect Cur in real samples of orange juice. Orange juice were collected and centrifuged at 12500 rpm for 20 min. Then, the supernatant was obtained and as-obtained orange juice samples were diluted by 10 times with deionized water. Subsequently, 0.368 mg of Cur was dissolved in 1 mL of the obtained orange juice supernatant to obtain a stock with concentration 1 mM. The NCQDs solution (200 μL) and 100 μL (300 μL or 500 μL) of 1 mM stock the Cur solution were mixed made up volume of 1 mL with PBS buffer solution. Finally, the mixture was taken for the emission spectrum (λex = 340 nm) measurement.

Practical application of a paper sensor for detection of Cur is explored. Here, curry powder weighed 0 mg, 29.4 mg, 59.0 mg were dissolved in 1 mL of the absolute ethanol, respectively. Then, the curry solution was detected according to the above paper sensor. Subsequently, the resulting test paper was compared with a standard color chart to estimate the concentration of Cur in the curry solution.

The morphology of NCQDs was characterized by TEM and XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared NCQDs were approximately spherical in shape, possessed good dispersibility and a uniform morphology. As shown in Fig. 1B, there is a weak diffraction peak at the position of 23° (2θ), indicating that the NCQDs is an amorphous structure. The surface functional groups of NCQDs were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1C). The absorption bands at 3400 cm−1 and 3177 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H; The band at 1668 cm-1 aroused from the characteristic absorption of CO. The peaks at 1581 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of CO/N–H and CO/O–H, respectively. The presence of –COOH, –OH, –NH2, and –CONH2 groups on the surface of NCQDs was demonstrated by using FTIR. The XPS spectrum of the full-scan survey of NCQDs was given by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique in Fig. 1D. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows three obvious peaks located at 284.8, 286.6, and 288.7 eV, which are corresponded to C–N, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively. The N 1s peaks at 400.0 and 401.5 eV in Fig. 1F could be attributed to C–N and N–H bonds, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) exhibits two peaks at 531.7 and 532.8 eV associated with C–O, and C–OH bands, respectively.

The optical properties of the obtained NCQDs were characterized by UV/vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. A strong absorption peak at 330 nm was observed in the UV/vis absorption spectrum of NCQDs (Fig. 2 A), which can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO groups on the surface of NCQDs. Meanwhile, Fig. 2B depicts the excitation and emission of fluorescence spectra, which the excitation wavelength and the emission wavelength were located 340 nm and 450 nm, respectively, while it emits blue fluorescence under 365 nm. When the excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence peak invariably remains at 450 nm (Fig. 2C). The NCQDs displayed an excitation-independent feature could be attributed to the effect of surface passivation functionality [26]. Nonetheless, as shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply after adding Cur, which was due to the FRET between the NCQDs and Cur.

As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs remained constant under continuous irradiation for 60 min or being stored for four months, indicating good photostability and structural stability. In addition, the fluorescence intensity increased when the pH was changed from 2.0 to 7.0, but decreased with further increase in pH (Fig. 3C). The fluorescence intensity increased gradually, when the pH changed from 2.0 to 7.0, which attributed to the presence of H+ in the solution and the presence of OH− on the surface of the NCQDs. However, the fluorescence intensity almost was quite stable in the pH range from 8.0 to 12.0. So the neutral pH 7.0 was selected in this work.

To comprehend the selectivity of the fluorescence assay towards Cur, as shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence response of the as prepared NCQDs to 0.1 mM Cur was compared with 1 mM of other similar substances which include 2,4-DCP, Tc, DA, ASA, 4-NP, PA, phenol, 2,4-DNP, Tyr, Asp, Cys, Cur. Under the same concentration, the NCQDs manifested specific recognition towards Cur, without apparent fluorescence quenching as observed in case of other standard substances, which suggested that the as-prepared NCQDs were promising and selective fluorescent probe for Cur.

The probable explanation for the strong quenching NCQDs by Cur could be the FRET between NCQDs and Cur, where the NCQDs are energy donors and Cur is the energy acceptor (Fig. 4B). To illuminate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by Cur, we firstly discussed the absorption band of Cur and the fluorescence spectra of NCQDs. It is obviously demonstrated from Fig. 4C that the complementary overlaps of the Cur absorption band with the NCQDs emission bands [27], and the emission wavelength of NCQDs is the excitation wavelength of Cur. In addition, folic acid can’t affect the fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence intensity of NCQDs (Fig. 4D (a), (b), (c)), Cur can quench the fluorescence of NCQDs (Fig. 4D (d)), and folic acid can enhance the fluorescence of Cur (Fig. 4D (e), (f)). Therefore, folic acid interacts with Cur to enhance the fluorescence intensity of Cur, whereas folic acid has no effect on the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs. Thus, it can be considered that folic acid interrupts the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between NCQDs and Cur, which supports the FRET mechanism between NCQD and Cur.

The analytical performance of the proposed fluorescence sensor to Cur was evaluated. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs at 450 nm decreased gradually with increasing concentrations of Cur, indicating that the fluorescence of NCQDs is effectively quenched by Cur (Fig. 5 ). The quenching efficiency (I/I0) presented good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9935) versus the concentration of Cur in the range of 0–2600 μM with a LOD of 0.13 μM (inset).

To further investigate the potential practical applications of this method, the sensing of Cur in orange juice samples was carried out. To verify the practical application of the proposed approach, different concentrations of Cur (100, 300 and 500 μM) are spiked in fruit juice. The result shows a good accuracy to our sensor with the high recovery in the range of 97.3%–115.0% for fruit juice samples in Table 1 . The coefficients of variation for the quantification are in the range of 0.15%–0.83%. The results indicate the practicability of the proposed sensing platform is very idea.

The visual detection of Cur is shown in Fig. 6 . The bright blue color gradually weakened in the NCQDs solution as the concentration of Cur with 0 μM, 300 μM, 600 μM, 900 μM, 1200 μM, 1500 μM, 1800 μM, 2100 μM, 2400 μM, respectively (Fig. 6A). However, as shown in Fig. 6B, an ideal paper sensor of Cur was realized with concentration of Cur for 0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM, 400 μM, 500 μM, 600 μM, respectively. When the concentration of Cur is larger, Cur shows its own color, so that paper sensing can’t be detected. And This paper sensor based on filter paper has good sensitivity to Cur (Fig. 6C). In addition, paper sensor response to Cur from real sample curry powder was tested in Fig. 6D. The results showed that the test paper achieved consistent results compared with the standard color chart, which the concentration of Cur was 100 μM, 200 μM, respectively and 0 μM as blank without the addition of Cur. Therefore, the content of Cur in this curry powder was determined to be 0.124%.

In summary, we have developed a facile hydrothermal synthetic approach for the preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCQDs) from citric acid and urea which gives high fluorescence signal. A new paper-based sensor of Cur by direct visualization is fabricated using the as-prepared NCQDs. The detection is fast, low cost, simple but highly sensitive. Significant fluorescence quenching of the NCQDs was found in response to Cur. The fluorescence quenching of NCQDs by Cur may be attributed to the FRET from NCQDs to Cur. The detection limit was determined to be 0.13 μM. This newly developed approach is attractive and promising for Cur detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575083) and the Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project (2015 H-W Li), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201801D121042), Transformation of Science and Technologyical Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2019KJ008) for the financial support on this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots: green synthesis and application as “off-on” fluorescent sensors for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate in biological samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Huang, Q. Li, Y. Chen, L.Tong, X. Lin, J. Zhu, Q. Tong, High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon dots: green synthesis and application as “off-on” fluorescent sensors for the determination of Fe3+ and adenosine triphosphate in biological samples, Sens. Actuators B: Chem. 276 (2018) 82-88.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and applications of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. T. Huang, X. F. Lin, F. M. Li, W. Weng, L. P. Lin, S. R. Hu, Synthesis and applications of carbon dots, Prog. In. Chem. 27 (2015) 1604-1614.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemically doped fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging, sensor, catalytic and photoelectronic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Du, S. Guo, Chemically doped fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging, sensor, catalytic and photoelectronic applications, Nanoscale 8 (2016) 2532-2543.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg (II) and I−

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. He, H. Zhang, J. Zou, Y. Liu, J. Zhuang, Y. Xiao, B. Lei, Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg (II) and I−, Biosens. Bioelectron. 79 (2016) 531-535.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots–quinoline derivative nanocomposite: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent chemosensor for detection of Cu 2+ ions in tap water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Li, Q. Huang, J. J. Zhu, W. G. Ji, Q. X. Tong, Carbon dots-quinoline derivative nanocomposite: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent chemosensor for detection of Cu 2+ ions in tap water, RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 87230-87236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Wang, Y. Wang, T. Guo, T. Yang, M. Chen, J. Wang, Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli, Biosens. Bioelectron. 85 (2016) 68-75.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots and derived 3D carbon nanoflowers are effective visible to near infrared fluorescent probes for hydrogen peroxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yang, F. Li, C. Zou, Q. Huang, D. Chen, Sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots and derived 3D carbon nanoflowers are effective visible to near infrared fluorescent probes for hydrogen peroxide, Microchim Acta. 184 (2017) 2055-2062.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free carbon quantum dots as photoluminescence probes for ultrasensitive detection of glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Qiao, H. Li, H. Li, J. Liu, W. Kong, Q. Hu, Z. Kang, Label-free carbon quantum dots as photoluminescence probes for ultrasensitive detection of glucose, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 69042-69046.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots from lemon juice with porphyrin-like structures surrounded by graphitic network for sensing applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. K. Mondal, A. Gupta, B. K. Shaw, S. Mondal, U. K. Ghorai, S. K. Saha, Highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots from lemon juice with porphyrin-like structures surrounded by graphitic network for sensing applications, RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 59927-59934.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sunset yellow in soft drinks based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yuan, X. Zhao, M. Qiao, J. Zhu, S. Liu, J. Yang, X. Hu, Determination of sunset yellow in soft drinks based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots, Spectrochim. Acta A 167 (2016) 106-110.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. P. Sun, B. Zhou, Y. Lin, W. Wang, K. S. Fernando, P. Pathak, M. J. Meziani, B. A. Harruff, X. Wang, H. F. Wang, H. Yang, M. E. Kose, B. L. Chen, L. Monica Veca, S. Y. Xie, P. G. Luo, Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 128 (2006) 7756-7757.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Navas Diaz, M. C. Ramos Peinado, Fluorometric determination of curcumin in yogurt and mustard, J. Agric. Food Chem. 40 (1992) 56-59.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intrinsically fluorescent nitrogen-containing carbon nanoparticles synthesized by a hydrothermal process

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. C. Yang, X. Li, J. Wang, Intrinsically fluorescent nitrogen-containing carbon nanoparticles synthesized by a hydrothermal process, Carbon. 49 (2011) 5207-5212.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots as fluorescence resonance energy transfer sensors for organophosphate pesticides determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. L. Wu, Y. Song, X. Yan, C. Z. Zhu, Y. Q. Ma, D. Du, Y. H. Lin, Carbon quantum dots as fluorescence resonance energy transfer sensors for organophosphate pesticides determination, Biosens. Bioelectron. 94 (2017), 292-297.
